paper_metadata.title: Majority support for global redistributive and climate policies
paper_metadata.authors: Adrien Fabre; Thomas Douenne; Linus Mattauch
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: 20-country global survey; western follow-up surveys in France, Germany, Spain, the UK and the USA
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: 40,680 respondents in 20 countries; 8,000 respondents in the western surveys
exact_tax_terms_used: global tax on GHG financing a global basic income
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Measures support for a global greenhouse-gas/carbon tax paired with equal cash transfers (a global basic income) and compares it with a quota-based alternative.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: partisan leaning / vote choice (Biden vs Trump)
determinants.definition: Respondent’s political orientation or partisan vote choice.
determinants.mechanism: More Democratic/left-leaning respondents are more supportive of redistributive climate taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the Global Climate Scheme (GCS): a global tax on greenhouse gases financing a global basic income.
determinants.evidence_quote: The strongest correlates are political leaning.
determinants.page_or_section: Results – Stated support for the GCS; Supplementary Section F
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 74% support among Biden voters versus 26% among Trump voters.
determinants.notes: Descriptive voter split reported in the main text.

determinants.definition: Confidence in government institutions and their ability to implement policy.
determinants.mechanism: Higher institutional trust reduces concerns about implementation and enforcement.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the Global Climate Scheme (GCS): a global tax on greenhouse gases financing a global basic income.
determinants.evidence_quote: The strongest correlates are political leaning, trust in the government and perceptions that climate policies are effective at reducing emissions or in one’s self-interest.
determinants.page_or_section: Results – Stated support for the GCS; Supplementary Section F

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: perceptions that climate policies are effective at reducing emissions
determinants.definition: Belief that climate policies effectively reduce emissions.
determinants.mechanism: Perceiving the tax as effective increases willingness to support it.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the Global Climate Scheme (GCS): a global tax on greenhouse gases financing a global basic income.
determinants.evidence_quote: The strongest correlates are ... perceptions that climate policies are effective at reducing emissions...
determinants.page_or_section: Results – Stated support for the GCS; Supplementary Section F

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: perceptions that climate policies are in one’s self-interest
determinants.definition: Belief that climate policy benefits the respondent personally.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived personal gains offset concern about tax costs.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the Global Climate Scheme (GCS): a global tax on greenhouse gases financing a global basic income.
determinants.evidence_quote: The strongest correlates are ... perceptions that climate policies are effective at reducing emissions or in one’s self-interest.
determinants.page_or_section: Results – Stated support for the GCS; Supplementary Section F

determinants.definition: Respondents were prompted to consider arguments for and against the GCS.
determinants.mechanism: Campaign-style exposure to counterarguments reduced stated support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: US2 treatment on support for the Global Climate Scheme (GCS).
determinants.evidence_quote: The support for the GCS decreased by 11 p.p. after respondents viewed a list of its pros and cons.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: -11 p.p. for the closed pros/cons list; -7 p.p. for the open-ended pros/cons prompt.
determinants.notes: Both closed and open-ended variants reduced support.
